slides.text: Hello everyone, and welcome. This presentation is on the digitalization of the model room, brought to you by Z R T I Bhusawal. Over the next few minutes, we will walk through the equipment, machines, and components that play a vital role in railway track maintenance. Let's get started.

We'll begin with the common equipment and machines that are used regularly on Indian Railways. These tools form the backbone of day-to-day track work, from welding and cutting to lifting and measuring.

First up, the portable generator. As the name suggests, this is a reliable power solution that field staff carry with them while working on the track. It provides electricity wherever needed, especially in locations where no mains supply is available.

Next, we have the air petrol or compressed air petrol oxygen LPG burner. This is used during preheating, which is the very first stage of aluminothermic welding, also called A-T welding. Proper preheating ensures a strong and defect-free weld.

This is the hydraulic rail weld trimmer. After an aluminothermic weld is completed, excess metal remains on top of the rail. The trimmer is used to neatly remove this extra metal, giving the rail a smooth running surface.

Here you see the welding mould. It is a prefabricated mould made from high silica sand conforming to I-S 1987 standard, mixed with sodium silicate. This mould is placed around the rail ends during A-T welding to hold the molten metal in shape.
This is the abrasive rail cutter disc. It is the cutting wheel that fits inside the abrasive rail cutting machine. Whenever a rail needs to be cut on the field, this disc does the actual job.

Now look at the rail jack. This is a mechanical jack used to lift the rail during ballast packing work. Since it works on a simple mechanical principle, it is easy to operate and maintain in the field.

Here is the hydraulic jack. It also lifts the rail for ballast packing, but instead of mechanical action, it works on the hydraulic principle. This gives it a higher lifting capacity with much less physical effort.
This is the hand grinder. It is a portable machine used to cut and grind uneven surfaces and burrs on the rail. It is very handy for finishing work after welding or cutting.
The rail drill machine is used to drill holes in the web of the rail. These holes are drilled to a specified size and are used for fitting fish plates and bolts at rail joints.
This is the abrasive rail cutter machine, the full machine in which the cutter disc is mounted. It is used to cut the rail directly on the field, quickly and accurately.

Here we have the lightweight rail-cum-road trolley. It is a specially designed trolley used for shifting small materials. The unique feature is that it can run both on the rail and on the road, making it very versatile.

This is the toe load measuring device, mechanical type. It is a specialized tool used in railway maintenance to measure the downward force exerted by the elastic rail clips, or E-R-Cs, on the rail. Correct toe load is essential to keep the rail firmly held to the sleeper.

Here is another view of the toe load measuring device. Just to recall, it measures the downward force exerted by the elastic rail clips on the rail. This reading helps us ensure that the clips are doing their job properly.

This is the cant board. It is generally used on curved sections of the track to indicate the amount of cant, that is, the elevation of the outer rail, provided on the curve. It helps the maintenance staff at a glance.
Here you see the skid, also known as the wooden sleeper skid. It is used to temporarily hold and support the rail on wooden sleepers, especially during repair and welding work.

This is the C-clamp, also called the rail clamp. It holds the rail ends and welds tightly in alignment. The C-clamp ensures that both rail ends remain level and in a straight line, so the joint comes out perfect without any mismatch.

This is the U-S-F-D inspection trolley. U-S-F-D stands for Ultrasonic Flaw Detection. This trolley is used to carry out the U-S-F-D inspection work, which detects hidden internal flaws inside the rail.

Here we have the slide chair. It is a specially shaped plate on which the tongue rail and the stock rail rest at a turnout. It acts as a bearing surface, holding the rails in correct level and alignment while allowing the tongue rail to slide smoothly.

This is the hydraulic jammed E-R-C extractor. It is typically used to remove elastic rail clips that have become seized or stuck inside the concrete sleepers. Without this tool, removing such jammed clips would be very difficult.

Here we see the A-C-B plate, that is, the anti-creep bearing plate. It is a metal key placed between the rail and the sleeper. It holds the rail firmly in position, supports it, and resists the longitudinal movement of the rail, which is also called creep.

These are hook bolts. A hook bolt is a specialised fastener used primarily on railway bridges. Its purpose is to secure the wooden sleepers tightly to the steel rail or the steel girder of the bridge.
Here you see the distance block. As the name says, these blocks are used to maintain the correct gauge, or the proper spacing, between the rails, particularly at points and crossings.

This is the tommy bar. It is a simple but very useful tool for many maintenance tasks. It is used for flangeway clearance, track alignment, and for applying extra torque while tightening or loosening bolts and nuts.
Now let's look at the various bolts and nuts used in railway track work. Each one has a specific purpose and is designed for a particular load condition.
These are crossing bolts. They come in different sizes and are used to hold the nose, wing rails, distance blocks, and spacers tightly together at the crossing portion of a turnout.
Here we have the rail spike. Rail spikes are metal fasteners used in railway track construction and maintenance. They secure the rails and tie plates to the sleepers, mostly wooden sleepers.

These are stud bolts. They are fasteners specially designed to secure rails, base plates, and sleeper components under heavy vibration and dynamic loads. The design ensures that the joint stays tight even after long service.
This is the box spanner. As you can guess from the shape, it is used to loosen and tighten bolts, including stud bolts. The box shape grips the nut from all sides, preventing slippage.
Here is the screw key extractor. It is used to drill holes in wooden sleepers, into which the spike is then fastened. This ensures the spike goes in cleanly without splitting the sleeper.

This is the nylon insulator. Railway insulators, also known as rail road insulators or rail nylon insulators, play an indispensable role. They electrically isolate conductive components and at the same time help maintain the structural integrity of the track assembly.

Here we have the continuous rail thermometer. It is an instrument used to measure the rail temperature on a continuous basis. Knowing the rail temperature is critical, especially for long welded rail and during destressing operations.

These are double shank spikes. They are used to fasten rails, base plates, chairs, and other fittings to wooden sleepers. The double shank design provides a stronger grip and better holding power in the timber sleeper compared to a single shank.
That completes the equipment section. We will now move on to the fastenings used on railway tracks. Thank you for watching this part of the presentation.
